tracking.controllers: Vive Controllers or Valve Index Controllers

knownIssues.title: 5K resolution is extremely GPU-demanding
knownIssues.description: At native resolution (2448x2448 per eye), the Vive Pro 2 needs a top-tier GPU. Many users must lower supersampling significantly.
knownIssues.workaround: Set SteamVR render resolution to 60-80% for GPUs below RTX 3080. Use 90Hz instead of 120Hz to reduce GPU load.

knownIssues.title: WiGig wireless adapter limits resolution
knownIssues.description: The Vive Wireless Adapter compresses the video stream, limiting effective resolution below the headset's native 5K.
knownIssues.workaround: For maximum clarity, use the wired DisplayPort connection. Wireless is best for games where freedom of movement matters more than pixel clarity.

optimizationTips.tip: Lower SteamVR render resolution to 60-80% for most GPUs
optimizationTips.simpleExplanation: The Vive Pro 2 has so many pixels that even powerful GPUs struggle at full resolution. Reducing it a bit gives much smoother gameplay.
optimizationTips.advancedExplanation: At 100% SS, the Vive Pro 2 renders at ~4896x4896 combined (24M pixels/frame). At 90Hz that's 2.16 Gpixels/s — beyond RTX 4080's comfortable throughput for most VR titles. Set SS to 70% (3427x3427 combined) for RTX 3070-class GPUs, 80% for RTX 3080+.

optimizationTips.tip: Ensure Link Box firmware is up to date
optimizationTips.simpleExplanation: The small box that connects your headset cable to your PC needs to have its software updated for best performance.
optimizationTips.advancedExplanation: The Vive Pro 2 Link Box handles DP→headset signal conversion. Outdated firmware can introduce frame pacing issues. Update via Vive Console → Devices → Link Box → Check for Updates.
